quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 8, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Purity Calculations and Mass-Mass Stoichiometry
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Percentage Purity, Mass of Pure Substance from Impure Samples, Mass-Mass Stoichiometry, Excess Reactant Problems, Solution Identification

questions.question: How many moles of ammonia (NH₃) would be formed by the combination of 22.5 mol of nitrogen gas with a large excess of hydrogen gas?
5.2 g of silver nitrate are stirred into a solution containing excess calcium chloride. How many grams of precipitate are formed?
questions.explanation:
2AgNO₃ + CaCl₂ → 2AgCl↓ + Ca(NO₃)₂
Molar mass AgNO₃ = 169.87 g/mol, AgCl = 143.32 g/mol
Moles AgNO₃ = 5.2/169.87 = 0.0306 mol
Mole ratio AgNO₃:AgCl = 2:2 = 1:1
Moles AgCl = 0.0306 mol
Mass AgCl = 0.0306 × 143.32 = 4.39 g

questions.question: What mass of pure potassium is contained in a 1.2 g piece of 82% pure potassium?
Calculate the percentage purity of a 6.8 g sample of aluminum containing 5.6 g of pure aluminum.
questions.explanation:
Percentage purity = (mass of pure substance / total mass) × 100%
= (5.6/6.8) × 100% = 82.4%

questions.question: Of the listed, select the aqueous solution.
questions.explanation: HCl is commonly sold and used as an aqueous solution (hydrochloric acid). NaCl is a solid, H₂O is the solvent itself, and H₂SO₄ is often concentrated but can be aqueous; however, HCl is most typically referred to as an aqueous solution.

questions.question: Analyzing the equation 2H₂(g) + O₂(g) → 2H₂O(g). How many moles of H₂O(g) are produced when 14.8 mol of O₂(g) react?
How many moles of carbon dioxide are produced when 18.0 mol of propane (C₃H₈) burns completely in excess oxygen?
A sample contains 15.0 g of sodium carbonate that is 90% pure. What mass of pure sodium carbonate is present?
What mass of magnesium oxide is formed when 12.0 g of magnesium reacts completely with excess oxygen?
questions.explanation:
2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO
Molar mass Mg = 24.3 g/mol, MgO = 40.3 g/mol
Moles Mg = 12.0/24.3 = 0.494 mol
Mole ratio Mg:MgO = 2:2 = 1:1
Moles MgO = 0.494 mol
Mass MgO = 0.494 × 40.3 = 19.9 g

questions.question: Calculate the percentage purity of a 10.0 g iron sample that contains 8.2 g of pure iron.
How many grams of calcium chloride are produced when 11.1 g of calcium reacts with excess hydrochloric acid?
questions.explanation:
Ca + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂
Molar mass Ca = 40.08 g/mol, CaCl₂ = 110.98 g/mol
Moles Ca = 11.1/40.08 = 0.277 mol
Mole ratio Ca:CaCl₂ = 1:1
Moles CaCl₂ = 0.277 mol
Mass CaCl₂ = 0.277 × 110.98 = 30.7 g

questions.question: How many moles of sulfur dioxide are produced when 9.6 mol of sulfur reacts with excess oxygen?
questions.explanation:
S + O₂ → SO₂
Mole ratio S:SO₂ = 1:1
Moles SO₂ = 9.6 mol

questions.question: A 5.0 g sample of zinc reacts with excess hydrochloric acid. How many grams of hydrogen gas are produced?
questions.explanation:
Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂
Molar mass Zn = 65.38 g/mol, H₂ = 2.016 g/mol
Moles Zn = 5.0/65.38 = 0.0765 mol
Mole ratio Zn:H₂ = 1:1
Moles H₂ = 0.0765 mol
Mass H₂ = 0.0765 × 2.016 = 0.154 g

questions.question: A solution contains 12.5 g of potassium hydroxide that is 85% pure. Calculate the mass of impurities present.
questions.explanation:
Purity = 85%, so impurity percentage = 100% - 85% = 15%
Mass of impurities = 12.5 g × 0.15 = 1.875 g ≈ 1.88 g

questions.question: What mass of pure copper is present in a 7.5 g sample that is 80% pure?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered purity calculations and mass-mass stoichiometry! 🧪⚖️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review percentage purity calculations and mole ratios.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on converting mass to moles correctly and identifying limiting reactants.
scoreMessages.0: Please review stoichiometric calculations, purity concepts, and mole ratio applications.
